school: The critical link between Ibrahim ibn Adham's ascetic tradition and the formal Chishti order; brought the Sufi path from Syria to Chisht (in modern Afghanistan) and trained Abu Ahmad Chishti, who gave the order its name; his transmission created the lineage through which Moinuddin Chishti would eventually take the path to India — making him the grandfather of the entire Indian Chishti tradition

Critical link in the Naqshbandi silsila between Bayazid Bistami and Yusuf Hamadani; known for his doctrine of universal spiritual hospitality — the inscription above his hospice door read: 'Whoever enters this house, give them bread; ask not about their faith'; his sayings were compiled in Nūr al-ʿUlūm and deeply influenced Rumi and other Persian mystics
Khorasan / Naqshbandi Lineage — Bridge figure between the Baghdad school and the Naqshbandiyya and Yeseviyya

Pivotal figure linking the Khorasani Sufi tradition to both the Naqshbandiyya and Yeseviyya orders; both Abdul Khaliq Ghujdawani (founder of the Khwajagan, direct precursor to Naqshbandiyya) and Ahmad Yesevi (founder of Yeseviyya) were his students; his school in Merv was a major centre of Islamic learning in Central Asia

The actual founder of the Suhrawardi Sufi school — his nephew Shihab al-Din al-Suhrawardi is more famous, but Abu Najib established the foundational framework; his Adab al-Muridin (Etiquette of the Seekers) was the first systematic manual for Sufi conduct and became the blueprint for subsequent Sufi orders; key link between the Baghdad Junayd school and the institutionalised Suhrawardiyya

books.title: Adab al-Muridin
books.note: First systematic manual of Sufi conduct — blueprint for all subsequent Sufi orders

school: Naqshbandiyya — Formulated the 8 foundational principles (kalimat-i qudsiyya) of the Naqshbandi path
Qadiriyya / Maghrebi Sufism — teacher of Ibn Arabi's masters; the most important Sufi of the Muslim West
quotes: Whoever truly seeks God finds that God was seeking him first.
The veil between you and God is not God — it is yourself.
Contentment is a treasure that never runs out.

school: Moroccan School / Shadhiliyya Founding — Murdered on his mountain; his student founded the Shadhiliyya

books.title: Al-Salat al-Mashishiyya (The Mashishi Prayer)
books.note: One of the most revered Sufi prayers in the world — recited daily by millions in the Shadhili tradition.

school: The spiritual master of Abu'l-Hasan al-Shadhili and thus the grandfather-figure of the entire Shadhiliyya order; his Salat al-Mashishiyya (Prayer of Ibn Mashish) is one of the most widely recited devotional prayers in the Islamic world, used daily by Shadhili followers across five continents; a descendant of the Prophet through Idris I of Morocco

books.title: Al-Salat al-Mashishiyya
books.note: The most celebrated Shadhili devotional prayer, recited globally

school: Akbarian — student of Ibn Arabi's circle; known for shamahid (witnessing beauty) as spiritual practice

Founder of the Suhrawardiyya order in South Asia (c. 1170–1262), based in Multan. Unlike the Chishti emphasis on poverty, he accepted state patronage and land grants, using wealth to fund Sufi institutions. His approach created a durable model for organised Sufism across the Punjab.

Chishti Sufi saint of Delhi (1173–1235) who brought the Chishti order from Ajmer to the capital. A disciple of Moinuddin Chishti, he was renowned for his devotion to mystical audition (sama’). According to tradition, he died during an ecstatic listening session. His shrine in Mehrauli, Delhi, has been a centre of pilgrimage for eight centuries.
quotes: The heart that loves God has no room for pride.
Service to the poor is service to God — there is no shorter path.
The disciple who serves his master has already begun to serve God.
The heart that loves God has no room for pride.
Service to the poor is service to God — there is no shorter path.
The disciple who serves his master has already begun to serve God.

school: Qalandari / Suhrawardi — antinomian Sufi; the 'dhammal' ecstatic dance at his shrine is world-famous
Chishti Order (Punjab) — Teacher of Nizamuddin Auliya; his Punjabi verses enshrined in the Sikh Guru Granth Sahib

books.title: Shaloks of Baba Farid (Punjabi verses)
books.note: His Punjabi verses are in the Sikh scripture (Guru Granth Sahib) — the only Muslim saint so honoured.

quotes: Pride is the veil between the servant and God — lower it and the light enters.
The dervish who needs nothing is richer than the king who has everything.
Whoever serves the creation has served the Creator.
Pride is the veil between the servant and God — lower it and the light enters.
The dervish who needs nothing is richer than the king who has everything.
Whoever serves the creation has served the Creator.

school: Wandering Dervish / Qalandari — Catalysed the founding of the Mawlawiyya

books.title: Maqalat-e Shams (Discourses of Shams)
books.note: Rumi named his entire Divan after Shams: the Divan-i Shams-i Tabrizi.

quotes: Past and future veil God from our sight — burn both of them with fire.
The message behind the words is what transforms — not the words themselves.
Whoever sees only himself in the mirror has not yet learned to look.

school: Chishti — third generation Chishti master in India; patron of simple living; refused all royal gifts
Chishti — earliest Sufi poet to write in Hindi (Hindawi); bridge between Arabic Sufism and Indian vernacular literature

One of the earliest Sufi missionaries in Bengal; established the Suhrawardiyya presence in eastern India decades before the Chishti order arrived; his khanqah at Pandua (near modern Malda) became the first major centre of Islamic learning in Bengal; credited with mass conversions through spiritual charisma; his tomb remains a major pilgrimage site

Cousin and principal khalifa of Baba Farid; founded the Sabiri branch of the Chishti order, known for its intense austerity and Jalali temperament contrasting with the Nizami branch's accessibility; his shrine at Kalyar Sharif near Roorkee remains one of the most visited Sufi shrines in India; his spiritual lineage continued through figures like Ashraf Jahangir Semnani
Shadhiliyya Order (Alexandria) — Direct student and successor of Abu al-Hasan al-Shadhili

Greatest Chishti saint of Delhi (1238–1325) whose spiritual influence shaped the culture and politics of the Delhi Sultanate. Teacher of the poet Amir Khusrow, he emphasised love, compassion, and service to all regardless of religion. His shrine in Delhi is one of the most visited in the Islamic world.

books.title: Fawa'id al-Fu'ad (Morals for the Heart)
books.note: First malfuzat (recorded spiritual conversations) in Persian literature.

quotes: If there is a Paradise on this earth, it is here, it is here, it is here.
Love for God is the foundation — and everything else is built upon it.
The heart is the throne of God — do not let anyone other than God sit upon it.
Humility is the crown of the saints — whoever wears it has already arrived.
Serve all people regardless of their faith — God’s mercy is wider than any religion.
The heart is the throne of God — do not let anyone other than God sit upon it.
Humility is the crown of the saints — whoever wears it has already arrived.
Serve all people regardless of their faith — God’s mercy is wider than any religion.

school: Suhrawardiyya Order (Multan) — His mausoleum in Multan is a UNESCO-listed masterpiece of Tughlaq architecture
Chishti Order (Delhi) — Student and successor of Nizamuddin Auliya; his death marked the end of the Delhi Chishti golden age

books.title: Khair al-Majalis (The Best of Gatherings — malfuzat)

school: Suhrawardiyya Order (Uch Sharif) — Descendant of Baha-ud-din Zakariya; travelled from Arabia to China
Naqshbandiyya — The critical teacher who transmitted the tradition directly to the order's founder
Kubrawiyya-Hamadaniyya — three times to Kashmir; converted the Kashmiri rulers to Islam; called 'Bab ul-Islam of Kashmir'
Chishti Order (Deccan) — Brought Chishti tradition to the Deccan; most prolific Chishti writer in Urdu and Persian
Shadhiliyya — greatest Moroccan Sufi of the 14th century; his letters on spiritual trial shaped Islamic mystical psychology
Shadhiliyya-Wafa'iyya — son of Muhammad Wafa; led the Egyptian Shadhili tradition; famous for mystical poetry
Shadhiliyya-Jazuliyya — revived the Shadhili order in Morocco; wrote the Dala'il al-Khayrat, the most printed Islamic text after the Quran
Naqshbandiyya — most powerful Naqshbandi master of the Timurid era; de facto ruler of Transoxiana through spiritual influence

The most politically influential Naqshbandi master; effectively the power behind the Timurid throne in Central Asia; his vast landholdings and network of followers made the Naqshbandiyya the dominant spiritual force from Transoxiana to Herat; his Faqarat is a key text of Naqshbandi spiritual practice; direct link between Baha-ud-Din Naqshband and Ahmad Sirhindi in the silsila
Naqshbandiyya — Balkan Sufi master; teacher of the Grand Vizier Sokollu Mehmed Pasha
Shadhiliyya / Khalwatiyya — prolific Sufi author who reconciled Islamic law and mysticism in Ottoman Egypt
quotes: The Sufi path and the Sharia are one road — whoever separates them has left both.
God does not withhold from the sincere seeker — only the insincere one is left empty.
The measure of a man’s love for God is the measure of his service to God’s creation.
The Sufi path and the Sharia are one road — whoever separates them has left both.
God does not withhold from the sincere seeker.
The measure of a man’s love for God is the measure of his service to God’s creation.

school: Shattariyya — major Sufi master of Mughal India; introduced the Shattariyya order widely; teacher of Tansen (Akbar's musician)
Qadiriyya — daughter of Mian Mir; one of the most celebrated female Sufis of Mughal India
Qadiriyya — major Qadiri saint of Lahore; his shrine is a pilgrimage site
Qadiriyya (Lahore) — Said to have laid the foundation stone of the Golden Temple of Amritsar; teacher of the Mughal prince Dara Shikoh
Naqshbandiyya (India) — Brought the Mujaddidi branch of Naqshbandiyya to India; his student Ahmad Sirhindi became its greatest figure
Khalwatiyya-Naqshbandiyya — greatest Sufi of the Makassar kingdom; exiled by the Dutch to Sri Lanka, then Cape Town where he spread Islam in South Africa
Naqshbandiyya-Mujaddidiyya (Delhi) — Martyred; assassinated 1781 for his view that Hindu scriptures contained divine wisdom

The primary successor of Mirza Mazhar Jan-e-Janan and one of the most influential Naqshbandi masters of 19th-century India; his Maqamat-i-Mazhari documents his teacher's spiritual stations; under his guidance the Delhi khanqah became the main centre transmitting the Mujaddidi branch of Naqshbandiyya; trained hundreds of khalifas who spread the order across India, the Arab world, and Southeast Asia

Influential reformist Sufi master whose students founded several major African orders; emphasised direct attachment to the Prophet (al-Tariqa al-Muhammadiyya) over affiliation to any particular silsila; his students founded the Sanusiyya (Muhammad al-Sanusi), the Khatmiyya (Muhammad Uthman al-Mirghani), and the Rashidiyya orders, reshaping Islam across Sudan, Libya, Somalia, and West Africa
quotes: The Prophet is the sun — we are the moons that reflect his light.
Purify the intention and you will find the action follows naturally.
Seek God in your every breath — for every breath that passes without remembrance is a loss.

school: Founded the Sanusiyya order in Cyrenaica (Libya) in 1837, establishing a network of zawiya lodges across the Sahara that served simultaneously as mosques, schools, law courts, and trading posts; his movement provided the organisational basis for Libyan resistance to Italian colonisation and later the Libyan state; combined Shadhili spirituality with Hanbali-influenced reform theology
quotes: The first obligation upon the Muslim is to know God — and to know God is to know His attributes.
Creed is the foundation — all acts of worship built on a cracked foundation will not stand.
The scholar who does not act on his knowledge is like a lamp that gives no light.

school: Algerian national hero, Qadiri Sufi master, and scholar (1808–1883) who led armed resistance against the French conquest of Algeria for fifteen years. After defeat and exile, he settled in Damascus where he saved thousands of Christians during the 1860 massacres. He was also a devoted student of Ibn Arabi’s philosophy.

books.title: Kitab al-Mawaqif (Book of Stoppings — 372 stations)
books.note: Written in exile in Damascus; one of the greatest mystical texts of the 19th century.

school: Chishti-Sabiri — The paradoxical fact that both Gangohi (Deoband) and Ahmad Raza Khan (Barelvi) were his disciples makes him Islam's most ecumenical 19th-century figure
Rifaiyya / Qadiriyya — major Sufi master of Bengal; spread the Rifai and Qadiri orders widely in East India

Qadiri and Chishti Sufi saint of Golra Sharif, near Islamabad (1859–1937). He was known for his scholarship, his defence of the Prophet’s honour, and his interfaith debates. His shrine remains one of the most visited in Pakistan, and he is revered for his miracles and his mastery of Persian and Arabic poetry.
Shadhiliyya-Alawiyya — Swiss metaphysician who founded the Traditionalist/Perennialist school; initiated by Ahmad al-Alawi

Abu Abdullah Muhammad al-Arabi al-Darqawi (Arabic: محمد العربي الدرقاوي; 1760–1823) was a Moroccan Sufi leader of the Shadhili tariqa and the author of letters concerning the dhikr he preached and instructions for daily life. He stressed noninvolvement in worldly affairs (Dunya) and spoke against other Sufi orders exploiting claims of barakah (blessings). He was imprisoned by the Moroccan ruler Mulay Slimane (r. 1792–1822) for supporting revolts against the throne, but was released by Abderrahmane (r. 1822–1859). A branch of the Shadhili order, the Darqawa, was organized around his teachings after his death, with members coming from a wide range of social groups. Though the Darqawa was once the most important tariqah in Morocco, its power waned as it spread throughout North Africa. His tomb is in the Zawiya Bou Brih also in the Rif.

Abū Bakr 'Abdollāh b. Moḥammad b. Šahāvar b. Anūšervān al-Rāzī (Persian: نجم‌الدین رازی) commonly known by the laqab, or sobriquet, of Najm al-Dīn Dāya, meaning "wetnurse" (573 AH/1177 – 654 AH/1256) was a 13th-century Sufi. Hamid Algar, translator of the Persian Merṣād to English, states the application of "wetnurse" to the author of the Merṣād derives from the idea of the initiate on the Path being a newborn infant who needs suckling to survive. Dāya followed the Sufi order, Kubrawiyya, established by one of his greatest influences, Najm al-Dīn Kubrā. Dāya traveled to Kārazm and soon became a morīd (pupil, one who follows the shaykh master and learns from him, undergoing spiritual training) of Najm al-Dīn Kubrā. Kubrā then appointed Shaikh Majd al-Dīn Bagdādī as the spiritual trainer who also became Dāya's biggest influence. Dāya constantly refers to al-Dīn Bagdādī as "our shaikh." When his master, Najm al-Dīn Kubrā, was murdered in 618/1221, Dāya fled to Hamadan, then to Ardabil, and then to Anatolia where he finally settled with a fellow contemporary master Rumi.

Imam Hassan Ukhra Muhammad Azeem Barkhiya (1898 – 27 January 1979) (Urdu: امام حسن اخرٰی محمد عظیم برخیا), commonly referred to as Qalandar Baba Auliya (Urdu: قلندر بابا اولیاء), was the founding Imam and Spiritual Master of the Azeemiyya Sufi Order, which takes its name from him. He was a Scholar of Tassawuf, and is considered to be of the Abdāl, commonly being known by his honorific "Abdāl Haqq" (Urdu: ابدال حق). He was a student of Tajuddin Baba. He appointed Khawaja Shamsuddin Azeemi as head of the Silsila, and passed on 27 January 1979. Every January, his Urs (Death Anniversary) is conducted in which members of the Silsila congregate to recite the Quran and conduct gathering on matters of Tassawuf. His resting place is located in the Shadman district of Karachi, Pakistan.

Sayyid Ali Hamadani was a Persian Sufi master of the Kubrawiyya order who played a pivotal role in the spread of Islam in Kashmir. He travelled with hundreds of disciples to the Kashmir valley, where he established mosques and khanqahs. His numerous works on Sufism, ethics, and governance earned him the title 'Shah-i Hamadan' (King of Hamadan).

Khwaja Usman Harooni was a major Sufi master of the Chishti order. He was the spiritual teacher of Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti, who would go on to establish the Chishti order in India. His emphasis on love, service, and spiritual poverty defined the character of the Chishti path for generations of followers.

Yahya al-Shirvani al-Bakuvi was a Sufi master who founded the Khalwatiyya order, one of the most widespread Sufi tariqas in the Ottoman world. Based in Shirvan (modern Azerbaijan), he developed a systematic approach to spiritual retreat (khalwa) that became the hallmark of his order. The Khalwatiyya spread throughout Anatolia, the Balkans, Egypt, and North Africa.

Abd al-Razzaq al-Qashani was a Persian Sufi scholar and a leading expositor of Ibn Arabi's metaphysical system. His commentary on the Fusus al-Hikam and his Sufi lexicon 'Istilahat al-Sufiyya' became standard reference works in the Akbarian tradition. His mystical Quran commentary, long misattributed to Ibn Arabi himself, was widely circulated.

Baba Farid, or Fariduddin Ganjshakar, was a major Chishti Sufi saint whose spiritual influence shaped the religious landscape of Punjab. His Punjabi verses are among the earliest literary works in the language and were later included in the Sikh scripture Guru Granth Sahib. His shrine at Pakpattan remains one of the most visited in South Asia.

Jahanara Begum was the eldest daughter of Shah Jahan and a significant Sufi author in the Qadiriyya order. She wrote 'Munis al-Arwah,' a biography of Chishti saints, and 'Risala-i Sahibiyya,' an account of her initiation into the Qadiri path. She wielded considerable political influence as Padshah Begum and was a major patron of architecture and Sufi institutions in Mughal Delhi.

Mahmud Hudayi was a prominent Ottoman Sufi master of the Jelveti branch of the Khalwatiyya order. He served as spiritual advisor to several Ottoman sultans and authored numerous works on Sufi practice and theology in Turkish and Arabic. His tekke in Uskudar, Istanbul, remains an active centre of Sufi devotion.

Sheikh Ahmadou Bamba was a Senegalese Sufi saint and the founder of the Muridiyya order, one of the largest Sufi brotherhoods in West Africa. His emphasis on work ethic, devotion, and peaceful resistance to French colonialism made him a revered figure. The city of Touba, built around his mosque, is the spiritual capital of the Muridiyya and hosts an annual pilgrimage of millions.

Ahmad al-Tijani was an Algerian Sufi master who founded the Tijaniyya order, one of the most widespread Sufi brotherhoods in Africa. After receiving a vision of Prophet Muhammad, he established his order in Fez, Morocco. The Tijaniyya spread rapidly across West and North Africa and today claims tens of millions of followers, particularly in Senegal, Nigeria, and the Maghreb.

Ahmad al-Rifa'i was an Iraqi Sufi saint and the founder of the Rifa'iyya order, one of the oldest Sufi tariqas. Based in the marshlands of southern Iraq, he emphasised humility, service to the poor, and ecstatic devotional practices. The Rifa'iyya became one of the most widespread Sufi orders in the Arab world, Turkey, and the Balkans.

Zahed Gilani was a Persian Sufi master who founded the Zahediyya order in Gilan. He was the spiritual teacher of Safi al-Din Ardabili, whose descendants would go on to establish the Safavid dynasty and transform Iran. The Zahediyya-Safaviyya lineage represents one of the most consequential Sufi-political connections in Islamic history.
